text_a: Bundle branch block -- Aight, when we talkin' 'bout a bundle branch block, docs can spot it if the QRS complex on the ECG take longer than 120 ms. On the ECG, you gonna see a R wave hanging at the end in lead V1 and a dragged S wave in lead I. Now, if it's a left bundle branch block, that stretches the whole QRS, and mostly, it flip the heart's electric direction to the left. The ECG gonna show either a QS or rS complex in lead V1 and a straight R wave in lead I. Another thing that's normal with a bundle branch block is what they call appropriate T wave discordance. That means the T wave goes the opposite direction of the last swing of the QRS complex. Bundle branch block, especially the left side one, can mess up the heart's timing. When both left and right bundle branch blocks happen at the same time, that's when you get a total AV block.
